questions.question: What is the primary mechanism by which exposure therapy works?

options.text: It proves that your fears were completely irrational
Repeated contact with feared stimuli leads to habituation and reduces anxiety over time
It distracts you from anxiety by keeping you busy
It forces you to confront fears until you stop caring about them

questions.explanation: Correct. Exposure works through habituation: when you stay in contact with a feared stimulus without avoiding or using safety behaviors, your nervous system learns 'this isn't actually dangerous' and the anxiety response decreases. This requires repeated, graduated exposure — not one-time 'flooding.'

questions.question: Why is a fear hierarchy important in exposure therapy?

options.text: To prove you can handle the scariest situation first
To create a graduated ladder from least to most anxiety-provoking exposures
To avoid ever facing the most feared situation
To compare your fears to other people's fears

questions.explanation: Yes. A fear hierarchy organizes exposures from manageable (anxiety 30/100) to challenging (70-80/100). You start with lower-level exposures to build confidence and skills before moving up. Jumping to the top of the hierarchy creates overwhelming anxiety that doesn't lead to learning.

questions.question: What are safety behaviors, and why do they interfere with exposure therapy?

options.text: Safety behaviors are healthy coping skills that should always be used during exposure
Safety behaviors are subtle avoidance strategies that prevent you from learning the feared outcome won't happen
Safety behaviors are unnecessary — exposure therapy has no risks
Safety behaviors are only a problem if you use them more than once

questions.explanation: Correct. Safety behaviors (gripping the armrest on a plane, rehearsing what to say, checking your phone during a social event) maintain the belief that 'I only survived because I used the safety behavior.' True habituation requires facing the fear without these crutches, so your brain learns 'I'm safe even without them.'

questions.question: SAFETY: When should exposure therapy be conducted with clinical supervision rather than self-directed?

options.text: Exposure therapy is always safe to do on your own for any fear
For trauma-related fears, severe phobias, OCD, or situations requiring specialized protocols
Only if you have a diagnosed anxiety disorder
Whenever you feel uncomfortable — all exposure should be supervised

questions.explanation: CRITICAL. Trauma exposure requires specialized protocols (prolonged exposure, EMDR) to prevent retraumatization. OCD requires response prevention, not reassurance. Severe phobias (driving after an accident) may need graded in-vivo support. For these, work with a trained clinician. Self-directed exposure is appropriate for mild-moderate fears (public speaking, social anxiety).

questions.question: Create a fear hierarchy for a mild-moderate fear you'd like to work on (NOT trauma, OCD, or severe phobia). List 5-8 steps from least (30/100 anxiety) to most challenging (70-80/100). What would be a good first exposure to start with?
